lines.text: I'm positioned on East Johnson, about two hundred meters from the Atwood Avenue intersection. Madison-wide count at {{ELAPSED}} is one hundred and thirty-nine confirmed. That's up seventeen from this point last year.

East Side is carrying forty-one percent of the load tonight — twenty-three kills clustered between Sherman and Eastmorland. Hottest zone by a significant margin. Weapons-trade data suggests firearms are down, bladed weapons up. Economic pressure.
Tucker, that's a nine percent shift in method from the first four hours. Can you correlate that to any specific incident or just general resource exhaustion?

Holden, the data doesn't distinguish causation at this resolution. What I can tell you is ammunition resupply patterns on the Beltline suggest a shortage around 00:30. After that, the substitution rate climbs. Could be Vance-era tariffs finally bottoming out the supply. Could be random.

Willy Street neighborhood watch — the barricade that held at the bridge — is still holding. Zero internal casualties reported. Their perimeter is actually tightening. I can see spotlights on the Yahara from here.
Any sign of pressure on that barricade as the night deepens? Sustained activity outside it?

Minimal. Activity's actually concentrating deeper into the East Side and... hold on, my equipment's registering — there's movement off Tenney Park, along the lake road, I'm seeing clusters move north toward Monroe and — wait, let me get a visual angle on —

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 139 cumulative at {{CLOCK}} | YoY +17 at this hour | East Side: 23 kills (41% of total)
Willy Street barricade holding steady | Tenney Park showing late-hour activity surge | Sherman-Eastmorland corridor remains critical
Weapons substitution (bladed/melee) up 9% YoY in hours 0-8 | Ammunition supply data suggests tariff impact
Tucker field-report cut at 02:07. Signal loss, East Johnson corridor. Standby for reconnect.
